element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Weapon selection becomes core strategy by mid-game
Advanced players create optimized loadouts; beginners use presets
Collision avoidance without enemy combat; navigation focus

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Mid-to-late game (SNES version repositioned from arcade)

element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Complex geometry and enemy patterns; climactic challenge

element_introduction_order.element: Hidden levels (based on Gradius I & Salamander)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Nostalgia content for series veterans; extreme difficulty

typical_relief_triggers: Stage 4 bonus level (no enemies, safe traversal)
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Arcade-style escalation with intensity peaks at boss encounters and late-stage sequences. Stage 4 acts as tension release valve. Arcade version is considerably more difficult than successors, creating relentless pressure; SNES version offers reduced difficulty option for pacing relief.

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Hidden levels accessible through progression; custom weapon edit available from start menu for expert players
spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Two hidden levels reference early Gradius and Salamander games; discovery rewards series knowledge

difficulty_curve.early_game: Tutorial-like introduction via Level 1-3 with Beginner Mode option (Japanese arcade/SNES). Teaches weapon selection and basic enemy patterns.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Complexity increases with dense enemy formations, varied weapon demands, and introduction of Stage 4 perspective shift. High-speed stage introduces timing pressure.
difficulty_curve.late_game: Crystal/Cell stage final sequence (repositioned in SNES port) combines all learned mechanics with extreme geometric complexity. No continuation feature in arcade/PSP versions creates permanent failure stakes.
notable_spikes: High-speed stage with Beacon boss (technical execution)
Arcade version overall difficulty spike vs. SNES port

notable_level_patterns.description: Early levels serve dual purpose: teaching enemy pattern recognition while funneling players toward weapon preference. Power-ups scattered across stage geography encourage exploration and decision-making.

Stage 4 breaks horizontal scrolling formula with third-person maze traversal. No enemy combat, only collision avoidance and free-floating power-up collection. Functions as difficulty release and pacing variation.
Mid-late game stage emphasizing speed and reaction time over complex positioning. Concludes with Beacon boss encounter that punishes hesitation.
Crystal/Cell stage uses complex spatial obstacles and tight corridors to force precise movement. Combines all previous mechanics under maximum environmental pressure.
Two unlockable stages reconstruct early Gradius and Salamander content. Serve as difficulty extremity content and series fan service.
